speaker: interviewee
text: Don't worry about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Because if you get warm, and the lights can get warm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm-- I'm fully aware of it. I can stand anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And just remember, I'll be guiding you along.

speaker: interviewee
text: I will-- I-- I-- want you to do that. If I stray off-- if I stray off--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, it's not really a conversational thing as much as it's a you talking with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: The date is April 1, 1996, survivor is Martin Selling, last name at birth is Selling. Interviewer is Louise Bobrow, in the city of Phoenix, the State is Arizona. The country is United States of America. And the language is in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Louise Bobrow. Today's date is April 1, 1996. I am conducting an interview with survivor, Martin Selling. The interview is being conducted in Phoenix, Arizona in the United States of America. Good morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your complete name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Martin I. Selling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell that please?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-E-L-L-I-N-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell your first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Martin, M-A-R-T-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is the I an initial, or does it stand for anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: It stands for Ignac.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell that please?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-G-N-A-C.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: January 1, 1918.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your present age?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Lehrberg, which is a little town in Bavaria.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell Lehrberg, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: L-E-H-R-B-E-R-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is this in Bavaria?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it's near Ludberg, or actually close to Ansbach, which is the nearest town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you describe Lehrberg? When you were growing up, what-- what was the approximate population?

speaker: interviewee
text: The population was about 1,000. It was practically one street, very long street between the mountain and the river.

speaker: interviewer
text: What part of Germany, if you were going to describe its location, north, south, central, Lehrberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: South.

speaker: interviewer
text: South. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Population was 1,000, you said. Out of the 1000, how many people were Jewish, approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were two families, my father's and my brother's-- and his brother's.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it an agricultural town, a commercial town?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was agricultural.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any other description you can give us of the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very ordinary looking.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said it was near what large city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nuremberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how far would it be from Nuremberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: 25 Miles.

speaker: interviewer
text: East, west?

speaker: interviewee
text: Southwest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lehrberg is the town that you grew up in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I would rather call it a village.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the home in which you grew up in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, very much, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: What street was it on?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was on the main street. There was only one street.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you want to take a picture?

speaker: interviewer
text: We will do that a little bit later. But I want to know what--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a one family home, with a farm attached. So the business, the textile business was on the first floor, and we lived upstairs.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many rooms did the house have?

speaker: interviewee
text: About a dozen, if you consider every nook and cranny.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it have a yard?

speaker: interviewee
text: It had a yard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any other memories of the home? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Isidor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Selling, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a merchant. We had a business there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Textile.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was tall, fairly stout, and I only can describe him from pictures. He died when I was three, and I don't really remember him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his death due to?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had a heart attack while he was away on the road.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ida.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Laub.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: L-A-U-B.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did she have an occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she was a housewife, and after my father died, she had to run the business.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also a tall person, very tall for that time, and fairly heavy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your relationship like with her, any memories?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was-- we had a very good relationship. But unfortunately, when we were 10, she started to become sick, and ultimately, the roles became reversed, and I had to take care of her.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her illness due to?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was later diagnosed as Parkinson, which was fairly unknown at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did she live?

speaker: interviewee
text: With-- till we were 18.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she die before--

speaker: interviewee
text: She died in 1936.

speaker: interviewee
text: A natural death.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were still in the same home when she--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a twin brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leopold. And later on, it was-- when we came to the States, he called himself Leo.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe Leopold, Leo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Similar to me, only darker.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your relationship like with your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good most of the time, but as twins are, sometimes we had different views and different characters. And we had to compensate for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was an identical twin?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any other-- early memories that-- of things you did together with your brother when you were youngsters?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did everything together. We had to. Our mother dressed us alike and kept us together. There were very few occasions when we were apart.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you didn't look anything alike?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, except in stature. And even so, we looked different. Here in United States later on, somebody who did not know he was my brother walked up to him and asked me if we were related.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were all dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were. Do you-- do you recall their names at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: On your mother's side?

speaker: interviewee
text: On both sides. I made a list of the family history, as much as I could from memory. Unfortunately, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very soon, when the Nazis came, we were not too interested in the background, and we didn't have time watch-- to do some research, even though I was interested. But when I came to the United States, much later, after my brother died, I wrote the history of the family as far as I remembered it and from other sources. And that goes quite a while back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you name your grandparents names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. From father's side was Leopold and Teresa. Her first-- her maiden name was [NON-ENGLISH]. From my mother's side, it was Meyer and Nanni Laub. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We-- actually, our family goes back up to 1800 and before. I have traced it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were they all from the same area of Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my father's side, they all came from the same area. He was born in Hornberg. And I think there are only Sellings there. He came-- he came-- in Germany, he came from Hornberg. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father's great grandfather, whose name was Isaac, lived-- I think was the first one who adopted the name Selling, because it was after 19-- after 1816, when the Jews in Bavaria could adopt name. How he got his name, I don't know. It's so English-sounding, nobody will believe me. On my mother's side, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] her mother's maiden name was Kramer, and they got the name 1816, same time, when Jews taken from Northern Bavaria, when those-- when the Jews were allowed to take family names. So local Catholic priest asked my ancestor to perpetuate his name by adopting it. And to reconcile with some other branches of the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: What schools did you attend in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Regular public school, of course and than after the fourth grade, we went to middle school, [NON-ENGLISH], and I was 16. It was in 1934, when the Nazis were in power, and I had to leave. I wanted to study further, but I couldn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these were public schools?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how many years of education did you have in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had only ten.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe your religious background?

speaker: interviewee
text: Personally, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very moderate and not very religious, and now I would call myself an atheist, more or less. But family history was fairly orthodox. Orthodox was the only Jewish religion in Germany at that time, especially in the small towns. There was also a reform congregation in Berlin and Frankfurt. And people were more or less orthodox, but they all had the orthodox education.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your town have a synagogue in it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were only two people. We had to go to Ansbach, there was a synagogue. There was-- it had about 25 to 40 members there. That synagogue there was built 1750 or thereabouts. When Frederick II, the King of Prussia, married his sister to the Marquis of Ansbach, he built a castle for them, a palace. And it's the same architect built that synagogue. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you recall any Jewish holidays being celebrated in your home or with your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they were all celebrated. Our business, of course, was closed for Jewish holidays and on Saturday. And-- but later on, we drifted a little bit away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any particular holiday stand out in mind? For example. Passover, do you have any recollections of celebrating Passover with the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Actually, the only-- the-- what stands out most is one of the earliest I remember. There was-- we had a tradition in our family that our father sat at the head of the table, and our mother sat next to him. And after he died, nobody was allowed to sit there for years. And about two years later, at the Seder with an uncle, at the Seder, I and my brother were extremely upset that somebody sat in our father's chair. That was the first time anybody sat there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was an uncle that sat there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a Friday night dinner, traditionally?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very rich [CHUCKLES] and very stuffing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was mostly meat, chicken, everything, everything with heavy sauces. We usually had wine with it. And the whole family life more or less centered around eating.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any particular food that you remember having for Friday night sabbath dinner?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but usually it was the same. It was-- there wasn't so much choice. It was-- we didn't have the stress on variety as we have here. So more or less the same thing was cooked every week.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any other holidays that come to mind, Hanukkah, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we lighted the candles and-- that-- [INAUDIBLE] but as our mother became sick, social occasions dwindled. We-- Nothing was there to do it, and so it just fell on the wayside.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you close to any other relatives in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And that also is maybe typically of the fact that our father was dead, our mother gravitated more to her side of the family, and so relationship with our mother's family was much more intense than with our father's family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any particular relative that you were close to on your mother's side?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually to all of them, I hated them equally, [CHUCKLES] most of them, at least. Because what was a fact of life was here we were, the poor little orphans, and everybody wanted to have a part in our education.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were pulled left and right and everything, and they-- whatever we did should have been done better. And we were regimented more than our cousins were. It was at least-- it wasn't a pleasant childhood. Did you have a bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you recall about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too much. It was traditional. And it was kind of stilted, because two sides of the families met, and there was a little friction. There always is friction. Primarily where there is inheritance, there is disagreement. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the relatives of my father were-- had part of the business. And when-- after he died, it was supposed to be split up, there were disagreements. And they carried through.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe yourself as a youngster? What were you like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Introverted [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and mostly lonely.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you-- what did you do for fun? What were your activities?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have much fun. It's-- somehow I don't remember funny thing-- fun things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any particular hobbies or interests?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I read a lot, because I was introverted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any dreams or ambitions of the future?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My ambition was to become a doctor. But that was quickly put aside after the Nazis came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have non-Jewish friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't have any friends. Being a twin, we were so interdependent on each other, that, at least I didn't need many outside friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when your mother died?

speaker: interviewer
text: So at that point, you were on your own, or did you-- did you live with anyone else?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had our old house. After our mother died, our uncle, who lived with us and ran our business continued to live with us. And even wherever we were, whether it was Wurzburg, or whether it was Munich, our home was Lehrberg. And we always came back. And we kept the housekeeper after our mother's death, and she rents the house for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any experiences of anti-Semitism personally prior to the rise of Hitler in Germany? Did anything happen to you personally?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was general. There was nothing too overt or nothing. It was accepted. Most countries, and Germany in particular, there was always latent anti-Semitism, just like in the United States and every place else. You just learned to live with it. And I took it for granted, just the way I took for granted that my father was dead. It's part of-- it was part of life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give me any example of anything specific that happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But it-- it was around. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That-- my-- we just were different, which-- which was accepted, and no fuss was made about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you recall anything at all about the 1936 Olympics that were held in Berlin? Did you want to go see it, or you were involved in any way in it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at all. See, there's one thing which people don't realize. That [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were Germans and felt like Germans. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] All our [INAUDIBLE], and including my father, were in World War I. The-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my mother had two brothers. We considered ourselves Germans. He was very patriotic. Even after he was wounded in World War I, he volunteered to go back to the front. It was his Germany. My uncle, too, he was part of being [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a veteran of the German wars. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] On my father's side, he had two brothers, one who died in Romania, and the other one who was wounded and later who had a head shot. He later developed epilepsy, and sooner or later, he-- he got more and more-- was more and more affected by it and afflicted by it. And he died from it in 1932. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's-- when we-- when Hitler came to power, it was a shock. I remember one thing very vividly, going to school, and I was told I had to sit in the back of the class. That was--

speaker: interviewer
text: When about that was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: was in 1933, after Hitler got to power. And during the last year, I was sitting in the back of the class. The teachers didn't talk to me too much. Some were indirectly sympathetic, but that was life. And when I was 16, I was told frankly, you had all your legal education. That's it. So I had to become a-- I had to learn a trade for immigration. I know-- being a businessman is no-- nothing to immigrate with, so I wanted-- also [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all those machine shops and all the factories, they were taken over by the Nazis. So only thing I really could become was a tailor. And so finally, I was apprenticed to a little tailor in Wurzburg. And that was my trade.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then when you learned this trade?

speaker: interviewee
text: I started out when I was 16. And that was in 1936.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned Hitler rose to power in 1933. And one of the changes right away that you mentioned was having to sit in the back of the class.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other changes did you notice in the beginning?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody talked to me. They were afraid to. We had a business that suddenly came to a standstill. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we just were outcast. And we never thought ourselves anything different than German. That really came as a shock. Maybe I-- if I-- in Poland for instance, there was-- was always quite a bit of anti-Semitism. And Jews were Jews. And considered a nationality, Jews. We were not. Our nationality was German. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Period.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any other ways that your life was affected in the beginning, anything else?

speaker: interviewee
text: I've said enough. [CHUCKLES] That's all I can say about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever personally see Hitler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, when I couldn't avoid it. I lived in Munich, and Hitler came frequently to Munich. When he crosses the street, everybody had to stand at attention and shout, Heil, Hitler. I tried to get to a side street like everybody else and tried to get busy someplace else to avoid him. It wasn't a matter of wanting to see him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in Germany during Crystal Nacht?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you recall? Where were you that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a tailor in Munich. And my boss, a Jew, suddenly left overnight and fled to Italy. So I had no other place to go. I went back home to Lehrberg-- Lehrberg. And I was there with my uncle. And in the morning, the next-- actually, it wasn't the night, it was the morning after, at 6 o'clock, they came and my uncle and I were both arrested and processed and sent to a prison in Ansbach.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was in Nuremberg with relatives, my other brother. He was working there. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when they left, they came to the house the night before, did a lot of damage, ruined it, broke tables, chairs, and everything, also the china. And-- but he was not arrested. I don't know why. Luckily. [INAUDIBLE]. When they left, after, when they wanted to have a cup of coffee, they had two cups left and one saucer. And the table legs, two legs were missing, but there was another one there, which didn't come from that table after they were through destroying everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Nuremberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened at your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't destroy anything. They didn't touch anything, but they arrested us.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the morning, at 6 o'clock.

speaker: interviewer
text: What date?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the 10th of-- of November.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year?

speaker: interviewer
text: So nothing was touched in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Except they swiped a few things, like a camera. And they took our car away and drove it off, and it was never returned.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear anything outside? Was anything-- did you know what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. And since we were only Jews, there-- there was no-- nothing could be warned. It was all very quiet.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened when they came to the door?

speaker: interviewee
text: They rang the door. They arrested us. I was still in bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is they?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were a group of SR men in proud uniforms.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they tell you you could take anything with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They didn't even know where-- where-- they didn't even know where we are going and when and for how long. All they were told, go and get those Jews. And bring them here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us to Ansbach, as I said, and some village children too. They put us all in a hall. And at about 3 o'clock in the afternoon, some women and children were let go. They could go home. Or everybody over 55. And the rest were put into the local prison.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the prison?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's district prison.

speaker: interviewer
text: In--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Ansbach.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Ansbach. How-- who-- how many people were you with when you were put into prison?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, we were about 15 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Men?

speaker: interviewee
text: Men, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the conditions like in the prison?

speaker: interviewee
text: Regular prison. It was not very modern. It was an old fashioned prison. It was clean. The facilities were not very good. But that's what it was. My uncle and I were in a cell together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were with your--

speaker: interviewee
text: My uncle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your uncle. How long did you remain in the prison?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two days. And then about half of them were released and the other half were shipped to Nuremberg, to the prisons there.
